content.text: Well-studied algorithms form the invisible plumbing beneath every agentic AI system. When a RAG pipeline re-ranks retrieved chunks, when a coding agent walks a repository tree, or when a research agent merges citations from five parallel tools, the work is handled by a small set of classical algorithms — sorting, binary search, BFS, DFS, k-way merge, and deduplication. Knowing these patterns, their complexity, and their Python library equivalents lets you build correct, efficient agents without reinventing solutions to already-solved problems.

is a finite, unambiguous sequence of steps that transforms an input into a correct output for every valid instance of a problem class. Unlike a heuristic, an algorithm has a provable correctness guarantee and a known worst-case cost expressed in Big-O notation.
In the context of agentic AI the most important families are:
— ordering collections by a key (relevance score, timestamp, priority).
— locating items efficiently: linear O(n) for unsorted data, binary O(log n) for sorted.
— BFS and DFS over dependency graphs, task graphs, and file trees.
— combining already-sorted or already-scored streams without re-sorting the whole.
— extracting the k best items without a full sort.
— removing duplicate records by a canonical key before further processing.
These six families cover the vast majority of data-manipulation tasks that appear inside agent loops, retrieval pipelines, and orchestration layers.

LLM inference is expensive and latency-sensitive. Every token in the context window must earn its place. The algorithms above directly control which information reaches the model and in what order — wrong choices here degrade answer quality before the model sees a single token.

Consider a RAG agent with 500 retrieved chunks and a 16 k-token context budget. A naive approach passes the first 16 k tokens of raw retrieval output. A correct approach deduplicates by document ID, ranks by relevance score, and greedily fills the budget — producing a context 20–40 % more relevant at identical cost. That quality gap comes entirely from applying standard algorithms correctly.

Beyond retrieval, graph traversal appears whenever an agent reasons about structure: import dependency graphs for a coding agent, sub-task dependency graphs for a planner, knowledge-graph neighbourhoods for a question-answering agent. Without correct BFS/DFS the agent either misses reachable nodes or loops forever.
— every algorithm here has published proofs covering edge cases (empty input, duplicate keys, cycles in graphs, ties in rankings).
— Big-O bounds let you reason about performance before running a single benchmark.
are implemented in C and outperform handwritten Python loops by 5–20x.
— deduplicate first, then sort, then merge: each stage is independently testable and replaceable.
— 'top-k by score using a min-heap' is unambiguous to every engineer, reducing review friction.
when only the single maximum is needed wastes O(n log n) time;
— binary search silently returns a wrong index if the input list is not sorted. No exception is raised.
— Python's Timsort is stable, but mixing sort keys or coercing types breaks determinism in tests.
— recursive DFS without a visited set diverges on cyclic graphs; iterative BFS with a seen set does not.
— building a full sorted copy of a large list consumes O(n) extra memory; in-place sort avoids the copy but destroys the original order.
— merging k sorted streams with a heap costs O(n log k) per element; for k=1 or k=2 a simple two-pointer is faster in practice.

Python's built-in sort uses Timsort, a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort. It detects already-ordered 'runs' in the input and merges them, giving O(n) best-case time on partially sorted data and O(n log n) worst case. The
parameter extracts a comparison value once per element, so even complex key functions pay a single extraction cost.
|  Space: O(n) for the merge buffer  |  Stable: yes

To find the k largest items in a stream of n elements without sorting everything, maintain a min-heap of size k. Push each element; when the heap exceeds k, pop the smallest. After processing all n elements the heap holds the k largest.
For k << n this is substantially faster than a full O(n log n) sort. Python's
implements exactly this algorithm behind the scenes.
Given a sorted list of length n, binary search locates a target in O(log n) comparisons by repeatedly halving the search interval. The invariant is that the target, if present, lies within
(first position where target could be inserted) and
BFS explores a graph level by level using a queue (
). It guarantees the shortest path (in terms of edge count) from the source to any reachable node. A
set prevents revisiting. Time and space are both O(V + E) where V is the number of vertices and E the number of edges.

DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It naturally produces a topological ordering of a DAG and finds all connected components. Recursive DFS is concise but limited by Python's default recursion depth (~1000). Iterative DFS with an explicit stack avoids the limit.

Merging k sorted sequences can be done optimally by maintaining a min-heap of size k where each entry is (value, stream_index, iterator). Pop the minimum, yield it, then push the next element from the same stream. Total cost is O(n log k) where n is the total number of elements across all streams.
The diagram below shows how the six algorithm families connect the raw data sources of an agentic pipeline to the final context window delivered to the LLM.

attrs.alt: Algorithm pipeline in an agentic AI system — from raw sources through deduplication, sort, search, merge and BFS/DFS to the context builder and LLM

content.text: Each column in the algorithm layer is independently applicable: deduplication runs first (by URL, doc ID, or content hash), sort/rank next, binary search on the now-sorted list for point lookups, k-way merge when fusing multiple already-ranked streams, and BFS/DFS when the data is a graph rather than a list.
Choosing the right algorithm requires matching the data's structure and the query's nature:
when you need the top-k retrieved chunks by relevance score. If n > 10 000 and k < 100, nlargest is noticeably faster.
when you maintain a sorted list of seen timestamps, doc IDs, or priority values and need O(log n) insertion-point queries.
when the graph is unweighted and you want the shortest-hop path — e.g., minimum number of sub-tasks to reach a goal in a planning graph.
when you need to visit all reachable nodes (component membership) or produce a topological order of task dependencies.
when fusing k already-sorted event streams (e.g., log lines from k parallel tool calls, sorted by timestamp).
before any ranking step — feeding duplicates into a sort wastes compute and inflates context with redundant content.

Avoid algorithms whose preconditions do not hold: binary search on an unsorted list, DFS without a visited set on a cyclic graph, or heapq.merge on unsorted iterables all produce silently wrong results.

The following function demonstrates the full pipeline for context preparation in a RAG agent: deduplicate by URL, rank by score, enforce a token budget, and return the selected chunks in ranked order.

import heapq
from typing import TypedDict

class Chunk(TypedDict):
    url: str
    text: str
    score: float
    tokens: int

def build_context(
    raw_hits: list[Chunk],
    token_budget: int,
    k: int = 20,
) -> list[Chunk]:
    """
    Full pipeline:
    1. Deduplicate by URL (keep highest-scoring copy).
    2. Select top-k by score using a min-heap.
    3. Trim to token budget greedily from highest score.
    Returns chunks in descending score order.
    """
    # --- Step 1: deduplication O(n) ---
    by_url: dict[str, Chunk] = {}
    for chunk in raw_hits:
        url = chunk["url"]
        if url not in by_url or chunk["score"] > by_url[url]["score"]:
            by_url[url] = chunk
    unique = list(by_url.values())

    # --- Step 2: top-k selection O(n log k) ---
    candidates = heapq.nlargest(k, unique, key=lambda c: c["score"])

    # --- Step 3: budget enforcement O(k) ---
    selected: list[Chunk] = []
    used = 0
    for chunk in candidates:          # already in descending score order
        if used + chunk["tokens"] > token_budget:
            continue                  # skip, don't break — smaller chunks may still fit
        selected.append(chunk)
        used += chunk["tokens"]

    return selected

) in step 3: a later chunk with fewer tokens might still fit even after a large chunk is skipped. This greedy approach is O(k) and good enough for k ≤ a few hundred; for larger k a knapsack DP would be theoretically optimal but rarely justified in practice.
Example 1 — BFS for shortest task path
An agent planner represents tasks as nodes in a dependency graph. BFS finds the minimum number of steps to reach a goal node from the current state.

from collections import deque

# graph[node] = list of nodes that can be reached in one step from node
def bfs_shortest_path(
    start: str,
    goal: str,
    graph: dict[str, list[str]],
) -> list[str] | None:
    """
    Returns shortest path from start to goal as a list of node names,
    or None if no path exists.
    Complexity: O(V + E)
    """
    if start == goal:
        return [start]

    parent: dict[str, str | None] = {start: None}
    queue: deque[str] = deque([start])

    while queue:
        node = queue.popleft()
        for neighbour in graph.get(node, []):
            if neighbour in parent:
                continue
            parent[neighbour] = node
            if neighbour == goal:
                # Reconstruct path
                path = []
                cur: str | None = goal
                while cur is not None:
                    path.append(cur)
                    cur = parent[cur]
                return list(reversed(path))
            queue.append(neighbour)

    return None   # no path found


# --- Demo ---
task_graph = {
    "start":      ["fetch_data", "load_tools"],
    "fetch_data": ["parse"],
    "load_tools": ["parse"],
    "parse":      ["summarise"],
    "summarise":  ["done"],
}
path = bfs_shortest_path("start", "done", task_graph)
print(path)
# ['start', 'fetch_data', 'parse', 'summarise', 'done']
Example 2 — k-way merge of scored tool outputs in JavaScript
A Node.js orchestrator fires three retrieval tools in parallel. Each returns results pre-sorted by descending score. The orchestrator merges them efficiently without re-sorting the combined array.

A production research agent queries three external search APIs in parallel. Each API returns up to 20 results. The agent must produce a deduplicated, relevance-ranked context of at most 10 chunks within a 4 000-token budget, then cite the sources.

import asyncio, heapq, hashlib
from typing import TypedDict

class Hit(TypedDict):
    url: str
    title: str
    snippet: str
    score: float
    tokens: int

async def research_context(query: str, budget: int = 4000) -> list[Hit]:
    # Simulate parallel API calls (replace with real async HTTP calls)
    async def call_api(name: str) -> list[Hit]:
        await asyncio.sleep(0)          # yield to event loop
        # Placeholder data — real code would call an API
        return [
            {"url": f"https://{name}.com/a", "title": f"{name} A",
             "snippet": "...", "score": 0.90 - ord(name[0]) * 0.001, "tokens": 300},
            {"url": f"https://{name}.com/b", "title": f"{name} B",
             "snippet": "...", "score": 0.75 - ord(name[0]) * 0.001, "tokens": 250},
        ]

    raw_lists = await asyncio.gather(
        call_api("alpha"),
        call_api("beta"),
        call_api("gamma"),
    )
    raw_hits: list[Hit] = [h for lst in raw_lists for h in lst]

    # --- 1. Deduplicate by URL ---
    by_url: dict[str, Hit] = {}
    for hit in raw_hits:
        url = hit["url"]
        if url not in by_url or hit["score"] > by_url[url]["score"]:
            by_url[url] = hit

    # --- 2. Top-10 by score ---
    candidates = heapq.nlargest(10, by_url.values(), key=lambda h: h["score"])

    # --- 3. Token budget ---
    selected: list[Hit] = []
    used = 0
    for hit in candidates:
        if used + hit["tokens"] <= budget:
            selected.append(hit)
            used += hit["tokens"]

    return selected

if __name__ == "__main__":
    results = asyncio.run(research_context("agentic AI algorithms"))
    for r in results:
        print(f"  [{r['score']:.2f}] {r['url']}")

At scale this pattern handles hundreds of parallel tool results. The deduplication step is especially important because different search APIs frequently return the same high-authority URLs, and feeding duplicate content to the LLM wastes tokens and biases the answer toward whatever document happened to appear multiple times.
Sorting by a string instead of a float
Scores stored as strings sort lexicographically: '9' > '10' because '9' > '1'. Always coerce before sorting.

# Wrong — string comparison
hits.sort(key=lambda h: h["score"])       # if score is '0.9', '0.10', ...

# Right — numeric comparison
hits.sort(key=lambda h: float(h["score"]), reverse=True)
DFS without a visited set on a cyclic graph
Using sort when only the maximum or minimum is needed

# Wrong — O(n log n)
best = sorted(chunks, key=lambda c: c["score"], reverse=True)[0]

# Right — O(n)
best = max(chunks, key=lambda c: c["score"])
Mutating the original list during sort

# Wrong — modifies the caller's list in place unexpectedly
def rank(docs):
    docs.sort(key=lambda d: d["score"], reverse=True)
    return docs[:5]

# Right — return a new sorted list
def rank(docs):
    return sorted(docs, key=lambda d: d["score"], reverse=True)[:5]
— dedup is O(n) and eliminates redundant work in every downstream step.
when k << n — the heap approach is faster than sorting the full list.
— appending and popping from both ends is O(1); Python lists are O(n) for
— even with a visited set, very wide graphs can exhaust memory in BFS; a depth limit caps the frontier.
— when tie-breaking matters (e.g., preserve insertion order among equal-score chunks), rely on Python's stable Timsort: sort by primary key first, then by secondary key.
— for n < 1000 the constant factor of a simple
call is smaller than the overhead of writing and maintaining a custom heap.
The table below summarises the complexity of each algorithm family and the practical breakeven points relevant to agentic pipelines.
n up to ~500 k chunks before latency is felt
k < n/10; roughly 10x faster than full sort for k=20, n=5000
Any sorted list; trivially fast even for n=10^7
Task graphs up to ~100 k nodes; dominated by Python overhead beyond that
k ≤ 50 parallel tool streams; negligible vs. I/O cost
Always; hash collisions rare in practice

For agentic workloads n is typically under 10 000 (the number of retrieved chunks or tool results in a single agent step). At this scale all six algorithms run in single-digit milliseconds in pure Python. Algorithmic choice matters more for correctness than raw throughput — a wrong algorithm produces wrong output regardless of speed.

At larger scale — thousands of agents running concurrently, each with its own retrieval pipeline — the aggregated CPU of per-request sorting becomes meaningful. At that point, offload ranking to a purpose-built approximate nearest-neighbour library (FAISS, ScaNN) rather than tuning Python sort.
— O(1) append and popleft for BFS queues.
— lazy truncation of a k-way merge without materialising the full stream.
— a Python sorted list that maintains O(log n) insertion and O(log n) search simultaneously; useful when items arrive incrementally.
— production-grade BFS, DFS, topological sort, and shortest-path algorithms over arbitrary graphs; add
— vectorised sort and top-k on arrays; argpartition is O(n) for the partition step, outperforming heapq for large numpy arrays.
Using networkx for agent task graphs

import networkx as nx

# Build a directed task dependency graph
G = nx.DiGraph()
G.add_edges_from([
    ("plan", "fetch_docs"),
    ("plan", "load_tools"),
    ("fetch_docs", "rerank"),
    ("load_tools", "rerank"),
    ("rerank", "generate"),
])

# Topological order (which tasks must complete before which)
order = list(nx.topological_sort(G))
print(order)
# ['plan', 'fetch_docs', 'load_tools', 'rerank', 'generate']
# (exact order of fetch_docs / load_tools may vary — both depend only on 'plan')

# BFS layers (for parallel scheduling)
levels = list(nx.bfs_layers(G, "plan"))
print(levels)
# [['plan'], ['fetch_docs', 'load_tools'], ['rerank'], ['generate']]
# Tasks at the same level can run in parallel
Classic algorithms are not the only options for ranking and retrieval in agentic systems. The table below positions them against specialised alternatives.
Any size up to ~500 k items
Vector similarity at millions of items
Sparse retrieval fallback or hybrid mode

In practice, production pipelines layer these: FAISS retrieves a candidate set, heapq.nlargest selects the top-k, and an LLM reranker rescores that small set. Each layer is justified by the cost/quality tradeoff it offers.
— if a sort key comes from external data (e.g., a user-supplied score field), validate its type before sorting. A non-numeric score raises
and halts the agent mid-task; catch and coerce at the boundary.
— a malicious tool could return a synthetic graph node that creates a cycle or inflates the graph to exhaust memory. Apply a node/edge count limit before running BFS/DFS on external data.

— agent tests that assert on the order of retrieved chunks can become flaky if equal-score items sort in non-deterministic order. Add a stable secondary key (e.g., URL) to guarantee identical ordering across runs.
— Python's default recursion limit is 1000. Recursive DFS on a file tree 2000 levels deep raises
. Use iterative DFS with an explicit stack for any graph of unknown depth.
— if the source of a graph is an external API, the graph may unexpectedly contain cycles even if the schema says it is a DAG. Always use a visited set, even when cycles are 'impossible'.

Think of the six algorithm families as a processing conveyor belt. Raw data arrives dirty: duplicates, inconsistent scores, mixed ordering, and hidden structure. Each algorithm is a specialised station that removes one kind of mess before handing work to the next station:
— establishes quality order (Timsort, heap, O(n log n) or O(n log k))
— point lookups on the now-ordered result (bisect, O(log n))
— fuses multiple already-ordered streams without restarting (k-way heap, O(n log k))
— navigates structure rather than order (deque / stack, O(V + E))

The key insight: each station has a precondition (sorted input for binary search, acyclic or visited-tracked graph for DFS) and a postcondition (deduplicated set, k best items, shortest path). Violating a precondition produces wrong answers silently — which is why precondition validation belongs at the entry point of every pipeline function.
Always deduplicate by a canonical key before ranking — feeding duplicates to a sort inflates context and biases LLM answers.
instead of a full sort when k << n; it runs in O(n log k) rather than O(n log n).
Binary search requires a sorted input —
returns a plausible-but-wrong index on unsorted data without raising an error.
for BFS queues and always maintain a visited set —
is O(n) and unguarded DFS loops on cyclic graphs.
fuses k sorted streams in O(n log k) — far cheaper than concatenating and re-sorting.
For graphs of unknown provenance, enforce both a visited set and a node/edge count limit to prevent memory exhaustion from adversarial inputs.
At agentic-workload scale (n < 10 000) all six algorithms complete in milliseconds — choose for correctness first, then optimise only if profiling reveals a bottleneck.
Layer algorithms strategically: FAISS for approximate retrieval, heapq for top-k selection, and an LLM cross-encoder for high-precision final reranking.
